content:
# Philippians Chapter 2 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 195-196 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/PHP_002_page_001.jpg, PHP_002_page_002.jpg

---

**1** So if being united with Christ means anything to you — if his love brings you any comfort, if the Spirit draws you into real fellowship, if you have any tenderness or compassion at all —

**2** then make my joy complete: be of one mind, share the same love, be united in soul and purpose.

**3** Never act out of selfish ambition or empty pride. Instead, with genuine humility, count others as more important than yourselves.

**4** Do not look out only for your own interests, but pay attention to the interests of others as well.

**5** Let the same mindset be in you that was in Christ Jesus:

**6** Who, though he existed in the very form of God,
did not regard equality with God
as something to be seized and exploited.

**7** Instead, he emptied himself completely,
taking on the form of a slave,
being born in human likeness.

**8** And being found in appearance as a man,
he humbled himself even further,
becoming obedient all the way to death —
yes, even death on a cross.

**9** And so God raised him to the highest place
and gave him the name
that stands above every name,

**10** so that at the name of Jesus
every knee should bow —
in heaven, on earth, and under the earth —

**11** and every tongue declare:
"Jesus Christ is Lord!"
to the glory of God the Father.

**12** And so, my dear friends — you who have always obeyed, not only when I was present but even more now in my absence — keep working out your salvation with reverence and trembling.

**13** For it is God himself who is at work in you, both to ignite your desire and to fuel your action, according to his good purpose.

**14** Do everything without grumbling or arguing,

**15** so that you may become blameless and pure, children of God without fault in a crooked and twisted generation, among whom you shine like stars blazing in the night sky,

**16** holding out the word of life. Then on the day of Christ I will have reason to boast that I did not run in vain or labor for nothing.

**17** But even if my life is poured out like a drink offering on the sacrifice and service that your faith produces, I rejoice — and I share that joy with all of you.

**18** In the same way, you too should rejoice, and share your joy with me.

**19** I trust in the Lord Jesus to send Timothy to you soon, so that I too may be encouraged when I learn how things are with you.

**20** I have no one else like him — someone who will genuinely care about your welfare.

**21** For everyone else is looking out for their own interests, not those of Jesus Christ.

**22** But you know his proven character: like a son serving alongside his father, he has worked with me in the cause of the gospel.

**23** So I hope to send him as soon as I can see how things turn out for me.

**24** And I am confident in the Lord that I myself will come to you before long.

**25** But for now I thought it necessary to send Epaphroditus back to you — my brother, my co-worker, my fellow soldier, your messenger and minister to my need —

**26** because he has been longing for all of you and was deeply distressed that you heard he was ill.

**27** And indeed he was ill — dangerously close to death. But God had mercy on him, and not only on him but also on me, sparing me sorrow upon sorrow.

**28** So I am all the more eager to send him, so that when you see him again you may rejoice, and my own anxiety may be eased.

**29** Welcome him in the Lord with great joy, and hold people like him in the highest honor,

**30** because he nearly died for the work of Christ, risking his very life to make up for the help you could not give me in person.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### The Christ Hymn (vv. 5-11)
This passage is widely recognized as an early Christian hymn, possibly pre-Pauline, and I have rendered it with poetic line breaks to honor its liturgical character. Several key translation decisions:

- **v. 6 — "something to be seized and exploited"** (Greek: *harpagmon*): The Sinaiticus reads ΑΡΠΑΓΜΟΝ. This rare word has been debated for centuries. It can mean either "something to be grasped at" (a prize not yet held) or "something to be exploited" (a privilege already possessed). The context of the hymn — moving from divine status to self-emptying — strongly supports the sense of *not exploiting a status already held*. I chose "seized and exploited" to capture both dimensions: Christ did not clutch at equality with God as a thing to leverage for his own advantage.

- **v. 7 — "emptied himself completely"** (Greek: *heauton ekenosen*): The Sinaiticus reads ΕΑΥΤΟΝ ΕΚΕΝΩΣΕΝ. The verb *kenoo* means to empty, to pour out, to make void. "Emptied himself completely" conveys the radical totality of this self-divestment more forcefully than the traditional "made himself nothing," which can sound abstract. The Greek is visceral — a pouring out.

- **v. 7 — "form of a slave"** (Greek: *morphen doulou*): I retained "slave" rather than softening to "servant." The Greek *doulos* denotes a bondservant with no rights — the extreme opposite of the divine *morphe* in v. 6. The contrast is the entire point of the hymn's downward arc.

- **v. 9 — "raised him to the highest place"** (Greek: *hyperypsosen*): The Sinaiticus reads ΥΠΕΡΥΨΩΣΕΝ — a compound verb with *hyper* (super, above) + *ypsoo* (to exalt). This is not mere exaltation but *super-exaltation*, raising above all raising. "The highest place" captures this superlative force.

### Other Notable Choices

- **v. 1 — Fourfold "if" construction**: Paul's rhetoric here is not expressing doubt but intensifying appeal — "if these things are real to you (and they are!), then..." I rendered the conditional clauses to carry that emotional weight rather than sounding hypothetical.

- **v. 13 — "ignite your desire and fuel your action"**: The Greek reads *to thelein kai to energein* — "the willing and the working." A dynamic rendering demands more than "to will and to work." God is the active force behind both the impulse and the execution, so I used language of fire and fuel to convey divine energizing.

- **v. 15 — "stars blazing in the night sky"**: The Greek *phosteres en kosmo* means "luminaries in the world/cosmos." The image is astronomical — heavenly lights against darkness. "Stars blazing in the night sky" recovers the visual power of the metaphor against the backdrop of a "crooked and twisted generation."

- **v. 17 — "poured out like a drink offering"**: The Greek *spendomai* refers specifically to a libation — wine poured over a sacrifice. Paul sees his possible martyrdom as an offering poured atop the Philippians' own faithful service. The image is vivid and sacrificial; I preserved it directly.

- **v. 30 — "risking his very life"**: The Sinaiticus reading here aligns with *paraboleusamenos* (a variant meaning "having gambled/risked"), conveying that Epaphroditus hazarded his life recklessly for the gospel. This is stronger than simply "not regarding" his life.

### Manuscript Observations
The Sinaiticus text of Philippians 2 is largely consistent with the critical text. The scriptio continua (continuous writing without word breaks) of the uncial hand is clearly visible across the columns. The Christ Hymn portion falls in the third column of the first page image, beginning at approximately Phil 2:5. The text continues through the second page image, where the final verses of chapter 2 appear in the first column (Phil 2:24ff).

— PNEUMA
